title: First Month Checklist: Keeping Track of What's Been Done
summary: By the first month, notifications and tasks start piling up — a simple tracking system prevents things from being missed or duplicated.
category: After a Loss I: The First Weeks
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: First Month Checklist: Keeping Track of What's Been Done | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: By the first month, notifications and tasks start piling up — a simple tracking system prevents things from being missed or duplicated.
whatYouNeedToKnow: A single running list (paper or digital) is more effective than memory.
Track what's done, what's pending, and what's blocked.
This is a marathon, not a sprint — pacing matters.

paragraphs: A single, centralized list — whether a notebook, spreadsheet, or a tool built for this — prevents both duplicated effort and missed steps, especially once several family members are involved in different tasks.

For each task, tracking not just "done" but also "waiting on something" or "blocked" (like waiting for a death certificate) keeps the picture accurate rather than looking falsely complete or falsely behind.

nextSteps: Set up one shared list, even a simple one.
Revisit the list weekly rather than daily, to avoid burnout.
disclaimer: There's no fixed timeline for finishing everything — most families take months, not weeks.
